pattern: Since only a handful of points ever get chosen, examining every possible selection directly is simpler and safer than trying to shortcut the underlying geometry.
hints: The number of points you must finally choose is always small, so consider what happens if you just examine every possible group of that size.
For each candidate group, the only quantity that matters is the smallest Manhattan distance between any two of its members.
Keep the best (largest) such smallest-distance value across every group you examine; nothing about the square's shape needs to be exploited directly.

edge_inputs.description: the four corners of the square, all must be picked
one extra point close to a corner forces a different choice of four
picking more points than the square's corners alone can supply
several points clustered tightly on one edge next to well-spaced points elsewhere
k equal to the total number of available points, no choice involved
points spread across all four edges, not just the corners

stress.description: a modest random scatter of boundary points (kept small since the reference checks every candidate group explicitly)
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    side = rng.randint(5, 50)
    boundary = set()
    for x in range(side + 1):
        boundary.add((x, 0))
        boundary.add((x, side))
    for y in range(side + 1):
        boundary.add((0, y))
        boundary.add((side, y))
    boundary = list(boundary)
    n = max(4, min(14, len(boundary)))
    pts = rng.sample(boundary, n)
    k = rng.randint(4, min(7, n))
    return [side, [list(p) for p in pts], k]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def maxDistance(self, side, points, k):
        # independent re-derivation: same brute-force idea, but built by
        # incrementally growing subsets and tracking the running minimum
        # pairwise distance instead of recomputing it from scratch each time
        n = len(points)
        best = [0]
        chosen = []
        def backtrack(start, cur_min):
            if len(chosen) == k:
                if cur_min > best[0]:
                    best[0] = cur_min
                return
            remaining = k - len(chosen)
            for i in range(start, n - remaining + 1):
                x1, y1 = points[i]
                new_min = cur_min
                for j in chosen:
                    x2, y2 = points[j]
                    d = abs(x1 - x2) + abs(y1 - y2)
                    if d < new_min:
                        new_min = d
                chosen.append(i)
                backtrack(i + 1, new_min)
                chosen.pop()
        backtrack(0, float('inf'))
        return best[0]

complexity.time: O(C(n,k) * k^2) -- exact search, kept to small inputs by design
